title: Hay-Meaken. Nunchen Time
content:
A.
Back here, but now, the jobber John
Come by, an' cried, "Well done, zing on,
I thought as I come down the hill,
An' heïŋ―rd your zongs a-ringïŋ―n sh'ill,
Who woudden like to come, an' fling
A peïŋ―ir o' prongs where you did zing?"
J.
Aye, aye, he woudden vind it plaïŋ―,
To work all day a-meïŋ―kïŋ―n hay,
Or pitchïŋ―n o't, to eïŋ―rms a-spread
By lwoaders, yards above his head,
'T'ud meïŋ―ke en wipe his drippïŋ―n brow.
A.
Or else a-reïŋ―kïŋ―n a'ter plow.
J.
Or workïŋ―n, wi' his nimble pick,
A-stiffled wi' the hay, at rick.
A.
Our Company would suit en best,
When we do teïŋ―ke our bit o' rest,
At nunch, a-gather'd here below
The sheïŋ―de theïŋ―se wide-bough'd woak do drow,
Where hissïŋ―n froth mid rise, an' float
In horns o' eïŋ―le, to wet his droat.
J.
Aye, if his swellïŋ―n han' could drag
A meat-slice vrom his dinner bag.
'T'ud meïŋ―ke the busy little chap
Look rather glum, to zee his lap
Wi' all his meal ov woone dry crowst,
An' vinny cheese so dry as dowst.
A.
Well, I dont grumble at my food,
'Tis wholesome, John, an' zoo 'tis good.
J.
Whose reïŋ―ke is that a-lyïŋ―n there?
Do look a bit the woo'se vor wear.
A.
Oh! I mus' get the man to meïŋ―ke
A tooth or two vor thik wold reïŋ―ke,
'Tis leïŋ―bor lost to strike a stroke
Wi' him, wi' ha'f his teeth a-broke.
J.
I should ha' thought your han' too fine
To break your reïŋ―ke, if I broke mine.
A.
The ramsclaws thin'd his wooden gum
O' two teeth here, an' here were zome
That broke off when I reïŋ―k'd a patch
O' groun' wi' Jimmy, vor a match:
An' here's a gap where woone or two
Wer broke by Simon's clumsy shoe,
An' when I gi'ed his poll a poke,
Vor better luck, another broke.
In what a veag have you a-swung
Your pick, though, John? His stem's a-sprung.
J.
When I an' Simon had a het
O' pookïŋ―n, yonder, vor a bet,
The prongs o'n gi'd a tump a poke,
An' then I vound the stem o'n broke,
But they do meïŋ―ke the stems o' picks
O' stuff so brittle as a kicks.
A.
There's poor wold Jeïŋ―ne, wi' wrinkled skin,
A-tellïŋ―n, wi' her peakïŋ―d chin,
Zome teïŋ―le ov her young days, poor soul.
Do meïŋ―ke the young-woones smile. 'Tis droll.
What is it? Stop, an' let's goo near.
I do like theïŋ―se wold teïŋ―les. Let's hear.
meaning:
"Hay-Meaken. Nunchen Time" is a quintessential example of William Barnes's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
